skill.name: run-train
skill.description: Rigor Train skill for deep learning research repositories. Use when a documented or selected training command should be run conservatively for startup verification, short-run verification, full kickoff, or resume, with command, config, seed, log, checkpoint, status, and metric evidence written to standardized `train_outputs/`. Do not use for environment setup, exploratory sweeps, speculative idea implementation, or end-to-end orchestration.
skill.summary: Run selected deep learning training commands and capture conservative training evidence.
skill.author: lllllllama

security_audit.summary: Static external command findings are partly confirmed because scripts/run_training.py executes a user-selected training command. Weak cryptography and Ruby backtick detections are false positives from prose, Markdown formatting, and file names. The skill is publishable with warnings for trusted repositories and explicit user-approved commands only.

medium_findings.title: Selected Training Command Execution
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: scripts/run_training.py executes the selected --command value with subprocess.run. It uses shlex.split and does not use shell=True, which reduces shell injection, but the selected command can still run arbitrary executables in the target repository.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess.run call receives arguments derived from the required --command input. This is intended behavior for a training runner, but it is still real command execution.

medium_findings.title: Shared Module Loaded Outside Skill Directory
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: scripts/write_outputs.py dynamically loads a shared writer module from a parent directory. No malicious intent is shown, but runtime behavior depends on code outside this skill package.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path construction and exec_module call are explicit. The risk is contextual because the path is layout-derived rather than attacker-controlled in the shown code.

medium_findings.title: References Outside Skill Package
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE with limited severity: SKILL.md instructs the agent to use parent-directory reference files. This can expand the trusted instruction surface beyond the reviewed skill files, although no prompt injection was found in the reviewed files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relative parent references are visible in SKILL.md. The security impact depends on the unreviewed external reference files, so confidence is medium.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: the flagged locations contain descriptions, policy prose, or an argparse description. No cryptographic algorithm use was found at these lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines do not call hash functions or cryptographic APIs. The scanner appears to have matched substrings in natural language or filenames.

low_findings.title: Ruby Backtick Static Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: the flagged Markdown lines use backticks for field names, file names, and references. No Ruby code or shell backtick execution exists at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The backticks are Markdown inline-code formatting, not executable Ruby syntax. The surrounding content is documentation.

low_findings.title: Git Status Collection Is Expected Repository Inspection
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for malicious reconnaissance: scripts/run_training.py runs fixed git status commands to record files changed by training. The arguments are hardcoded and support reproducibility evidence.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The git commands are fixed and local to the supplied repository. This still inspects repository state, but the purpose matches the skill description.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run on Selected Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill executes a user-selected training command through subprocess.run. This should only be used in trusted repositories with explicit user approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous pattern is direct and intentional. shlex.split lowers shell injection risk but does not remove execution risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic Module Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The output wrapper imports and executes a shared module by filesystem path. Reviewers should verify the shared module before publishing updates.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dynamic import is clear in the code. The missing shared module content prevents a stronger maliciousness conclusion.

content.value_statement: Training runs often fail without clear evidence about commands, checkpoints, and progress. This skill runs a selected command and organizes conservative training evidence in train_outputs.
actual_capabilities: Runs a documented training command in startup, short-run, kickoff, or resume mode.
Captures stdout, stderr, timeout status, return code, and launch errors.
Extracts observed steps, epochs, metrics, and checkpoint paths from logs.
Compares git status before and after a run to report touched files.
Produces structured training status for standardized train_outputs evidence.
limitations: It does not choose the research goal or design new experiments.
It requires a trusted repository and an explicit training command.
It cannot prove full reproduction success from a short verification run.
It depends on repository logs for metric and checkpoint extraction.

use_cases.title: Verify a Training Command Starts
use_cases.description: Run a short startup check and capture whether the selected command launches, logs progress, and reaches initial steps.

use_cases.title: Resume a Stopped Training Run
use_cases.description: Run a selected resume command and summarize checkpoint source, observed progress, and remaining blockers.

use_cases.title: Record Reproducibility Evidence
use_cases.description: Collect command, status, metric, checkpoint, and changed-file evidence for a training run review.

prompt_templates.title: Startup Verification
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check for a documented command
prompt_templates.prompt: Use run-train to run this selected training command for startup verification with a short timeout. Record command, logs, status, and any blockers in train_outputs.

prompt_templates.title: Short-Run Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Use run-train for a short-run verification of the selected command. Capture completed steps, observed metrics, checkpoint hints, and whether the run stayed comparable.

prompt_templates.title: Resume Verification
prompt_templates.prompt: Use run-train to verify this resume command. Record the checkpoint source, resume status, logs, touched files, and any evidence that training continued.

prompt_templates.title: Full Kickoff Monitoring
prompt_templates.prompt: Use run-train for a full kickoff with a finite monitoring window. Report the command, runtime assumptions, progress, metrics, checkpoints, and whether supervision should continue.

output_examples.input: Run a startup verification for a PyTorch training command with a two-minute timeout.
output_examples.output: A concise training summary showing the command, launch result, timeout scope, observed steps, log excerpts, and whether the run is blocked or partial.

output_examples.input: Resume training from an existing checkpoint and capture reproducibility evidence.
output: Resume status with checkpoint source and observed progress.
Metric and checkpoint hints extracted from logs.
Changed-file summary for files touched during the run.

best_practices: Use only commands that the user has selected and approved.
Run in trusted repositories with known datasets and checkpoints.
Treat short or timed-out runs as partial evidence, not proof of full success.
anti_patterns: Do not use it to discover or invent new training commands.
Do not run commands from untrusted repositories without inspection.
Do not present startup verification as a completed reproduction.

faq.question: Does this skill choose the training command?
faq.answer: No. It expects a documented or user-selected command before execution.

faq.question: Can it run full training jobs?
faq.answer: Yes, but it treats supervision as a finite monitoring window unless another plan exists.

faq.question: What evidence does it capture?
faq.answer: It captures command details, logs, progress hints, metrics, checkpoints, status, and touched files.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted repositories?
faq.answer: No. It executes selected commands and should be used only with trusted code and explicit approval.

faq.question: Does it install dependencies?
faq.answer: No. Environment setup and asset downloads are outside this skill boundary.

faq.question: Can it confirm a paper was reproduced?
faq.answer: Not by itself. It reports conservative run evidence and avoids claiming full success from partial runs.
